text: Look at the certificate nginx is actually configured to serve: `grep ssl_certificate /etc/nginx/conf.d/tls-app.conf` to find the file, then `sudo openssl x509 -in <that file> -noout -enddate` to see its expiry.
You can also check it live over the wire (this is closer to what monitoring actually does): `echo | openssl s_client -connect localhost:8443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -enddate`.

Generate a fresh self-signed certificate to replace it: `sudo openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes -days 365 -keyout /etc/nginx/ssl/site.key -out /etc/nginx/ssl/site.crt -subj '/CN=app.internal'`.
nginx doesn't pick up new certificate files until it's told to: `sudo service nginx reload` (or `restart`). Then re-check the live certificate's dates.
